summary: 10 concrete tools, apps, and references surfaced from 21 qualifying HN threads.
telegram_footer: 10 items surfaced | 21/21 stories scraped | history + run artifact updated

items.name: StreetComplete
items.source_story_title: StreetComplete: Fixing OpenStreetMap, one tiny quest at a time
items.summary: OpenStreetMap quest app for fixing nearby map details.
items.why_included: Commenters said they use it to map sidewalks, intersections, and neighborhood details.
items.evidence: Comments described using StreetComplete on dog walks and after moving neighborhoods, with one user pointing to OSM stats about its quests.

items.name: 30papers.com
items.source_story_title: 30papers.com – Ilya's 30 essential ML papers, in a beginner friendly format
items.summary: Beginner-friendly guide to Ilya's 30 essential ML papers.
items.why_included: Commenters liked the presentation and saw it as a useful reading list.
items.evidence: HN comments called the styling and animation cool in isolation, said the list presentation was nice, and suggested the papers themselves as a good reading path.

items.name: Davit
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Davit, a Apple Containers UI
items.summary: Apple Containers UI for managing local container stacks.
items.why_included: Commenters called it solid and wanted to try it immediately.
items.evidence: Users said Davit looks like a really solid app, praised the small binary and notarized build, and asked for menubar integration.

items.name: Herdr
items.source_story_title: Herdr: One terminal to rule them all
items.summary: Terminal for running and reconnecting to AI agent sessions.
items.why_included: Commenters said they already use it for multi-agent workflows.
items.evidence: One commenter said they have been using Herdr to run AI agents and can reconnect from multiple machines; others compared it to tmux and similar terminal layers.

items.name: MacSurf
items.summary: Classic Mac OS 9 browser bringing modern web tech to old hardware.
items.why_included: Commenters were eager to boot vintage Macs and test it.
items.evidence: HN users said they would fire up iMac G4s to try MacSurf, called the progress awesome, and praised the NetSurf lineage.

items.name: Jim's TrueType QR Code Font
items.summary: TrueType font that turns text into QR codes.
items.why_included: Commenters were impressed by the font hack and its novelty.
items.evidence: Comments called the font trick amazing, noted the explanation was missing, and reported that spacing can break the generated QR output on some devices.

items.name: Kokoro
items.source_story_title: Local, CPU-Friendly, High-Quality TTS (Text-to-Speech) with Kokoro
items.summary: Small open-weight TTS model that runs locally on CPU.
items.why_included: Commenters praised the size, voice quality, and side-project usefulness.
items.evidence: HN users said Kokoro is very nice, called it legitimately super good for a tiny model, and shared side projects built on top of it.

items.name: l
items.source_story_title: l: A new runtime for k and q
items.summary: Array-language runtime for k and q.
items.why_included: Commenters called it cool and wanted fair benchmarks against other runtimes.
items.evidence: Commenters described l as quite cool, asked for comparisons to other k runtimes, and praised the design space even while complaining about the single-letter naming.

items.name: PgDog
items.source_story_title: Why we built yet another Postgres connection pooler
items.summary: PostgreSQL pooler, load balancer, and sharder.
items.why_included: Commenters thought the architecture looked strong and featureful.
items.evidence: HN discussion said PgDog looks amazing on paper, highlighted notify/listen fixes and read-replica routing, and compared its direction to Vitess.

items.name: Astro
items.summary: Astro 7.0 release with leaner dependencies and dev-server workflow tweaks.
items.why_included: Commenters praised the dependency cuts and the developer workflow ideas.
items.evidence: Users liked the drop from 247 to 190 dependencies, said Astro plus Claude Code is great for building sites, and liked the logs/background-server approach.
